title: Webhook Event Generation

story.i_want_to: have webhook_event records created automatically in the same database transaction as the state change that triggers them
story.so_that: no events are lost due to process crashes between the state change and event creation, ensuring exactly-once event generation

rationale: Webhook reliability depends on event generation being atomic with the state change. If events are created after the transaction commits (e.g., via a GenServer or async process), a crash between the commit and event creation silently drops the event. By creating webhook_event records inside the same Ecto.Multi transaction, we guarantee that if the state change is persisted, the event is too. Delivery is then handled asynchronously by Oban (US-10.3).

acceptance_criteria.description: A webhook_events table exists with columns: id (uuid PK), tenant_id (uuid FK), webhook_id (uuid FK), event_type (string), payload (jsonb), status (enum: pending, delivered, failed, exhausted), attempts (integer, default 0), last_attempt_at (utc_datetime, nullable), delivered_at (utc_datetime, nullable), error (text, nullable), inserted_at (utc_datetime). RLS scoped by tenant_id.

When a story's agent_status changes, a webhook_event with event_type='story.status_changed' is created in the SAME Ecto.Multi transaction for every active webhook subscription that includes this event type and matches the project scope.
When a story's verified_status changes to 'verified', a webhook_event with event_type='story.verified' is created transactionally.
When a story's verified_status changes to 'rejected', a webhook_event with event_type='story.rejected' is created transactionally. If auto-reset triggers, a 'story.auto_reset' event is also created.
When all stories in an epic reach verified_status='verified', a webhook_event with event_type='epic.completed' is created.
When a new artifact report is submitted, a webhook_event with event_type='artifact.reported' is created transactionally.
When a new agent self-registers, a webhook_event with event_type='agent.registered' is created transactionally.
When a bulk import completes, a webhook_event with event_type='project.imported' is created transactionally.
Event payloads include relevant data: event_type, entity identifiers, old/new values, actor information, and a timestamp. Payload structure is consistent and documented.
Webhook events are only created for active webhooks (active=true) whose events list includes the event type and whose project_id filter matches (NULL matches all projects).

test_cases.name: story status change creates webhook events transactionally
steps: Query webhook_events for webhook_id and event_type='story.status_changed'
expected_results: A webhook_event record exists with status='pending'
Event payload includes story_id, old_status='pending', new_status='assigned', agent_id
Both the story update and webhook_event insert are in the same transaction (if story update fails, no event exists)

test_cases.name: story verification creates story.verified event
steps: POST /api/v1/stories/:id/verify with orchestrator API key and verification result
expected_results: Payload includes story_id, orchestrator_id, verification summary

test_cases.name: story rejection creates both rejected and auto_reset events
steps: POST /api/v1/stories/:id/reject with orchestrator API key, reason, and findings
expected_results: Two webhook_events created: one with event_type='story.rejected', one with event_type='story.auto_reset'
story.rejected payload includes story_id, orchestrator_id, reason, findings
story.auto_reset payload includes story_id and reason='rejected'

test_cases.name: inactive webhooks do not receive events
steps: Query webhook_events for the inactive webhook
expected_results: No webhook_event records exist for the inactive webhook

test_cases.name: project-scoped webhook only receives events for that project
preconditions: fixture(:webhook, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, events: ['story.status_changed'], project_id: project_a.id, active: true})
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic belonging to project_a})
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic belonging to project_b})
expected_results: webhook_event exists for the Project A story
No webhook_event exists for the Project B story

test_cases.name: webhook with unsubscribed event type does not receive events
expected_results: No webhook_event created (webhook subscribes to story.verified, not story.status_changed)

test_cases.name: multiple webhooks receive the same event
preconditions: fixture(:webhook, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, url: 'https://a.example.com', events: ['story.status_changed'], active: true})
fixture(:webhook, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, url: 'https://b.example.com', events: ['story.status_changed'], active: true})
expected_results: Two webhook_event records created, one for each webhook

test_cases.name: event generation is atomic with state change
steps: Execute the Ecto.Multi that claims a story but force a rollback (e.g., by adding a failing step after the webhook event insert)
Query both stories and webhook_events tables
expected_results: No webhook_event records exist (rolled back with the transaction)

test_cases.name: cross-tenant isolation — webhook events not visible to other tenants
steps: POST /api/v1/stories/:id/claim with Tenant A's agent API key
Query webhook_events for Tenant B's webhook
expected_results: No webhook_event records exist for Tenant B's webhook
webhook_event exists only for Tenant A's webhook (RLS ensures tenant isolation)

technical_notes: Create migration for webhook_events table with RLS policies (INSERT, SELECT scoped by tenant_id).
Create a Loopctl.Webhooks.EventGenerator module with a generate_events/3 function that accepts (multi, event_type, payload) and appends webhook_event inserts to the Ecto.Multi.
The generator must query active webhooks matching the event_type and project scope, then insert one webhook_event per matching webhook.

Integrate EventGenerator.generate_events into every Ecto.Multi pipeline that produces auditable state changes: Stories context (claim, start, report, verify, reject), Artifacts context, Agents context (register), Import context.

After the transaction commits, enqueue Oban jobs for each pending webhook_event. This can be done via an Ecto.Multi after-commit callback or an Oban insert_all in the multi (Oban jobs are transactional when inserted in the same multi).
Payload schema examples: story.status_changed: {event: 'story.status_changed', story_id, project_id, epic_id, old_status, new_status, agent_id, timestamp}.
The epic.completed event requires checking if all stories in the epic are verified after each story verification. This check runs inside the verify transaction.

For bulk operations (US-13.1), querying active webhooks inside Ecto.Multi for each story could result in N * W database queries (N stories * W webhooks). Consider caching active webhook subscriptions in ETS with a short TTL (e.g., 60 seconds) keyed by {tenant_id, project_id}. Invalidate on webhook create/update/delete.
Consider a helper function that wraps the common pattern: update entity + create audit log + generate webhook events, all in one Ecto.Multi.
All test modules MUST use `async: true` (e.g., `use Loopctl.DataCase, async: true` or `use LoopctlWeb.ConnCase, async: true`)
All tests using Mox MUST include `setup :verify_on_exit!`
All mocks MUST be defined centrally in `test/support/mocks.ex`, never in individual test files
Schema module MUST use `use Loopctl.Schema` (provides `@primary_key {:id, :binary_id, autogenerate: true}`, `@foreign_key_type :binary_id`, `@timestamps_opts [type: :utc_datetime_usec]`)
